data.title: Wall St futures steady as oil climbs, Iran peace talks remain stalled
data.description:
Investing.com- U.S. stock index futures were little changed on Sunday evening after record-high closes last week, as a surge in oil prices and stalled U.S.-Iran talks dampened risk appetite ahead of a packed week for Wall Street.
S&P 500 Futures inched 0.1% lower to 7,189.75 points, while Nasdaq 100 Futures gained 0.1% to 27,455.75 points by 20:50 ET (00:50 GMT). Dow Jones Futures fell 0.2% at 49,293.0 points.
[[pro_promotion |Get premium Wall Street insights with an InvestingPro subscription
]]
The S&P 500 and NASDAQ Composite closed at record highs on Friday, capping a fourth straight week of gains, supported by strength in megacap technology stocks.
For the week, the S&P 500 climbed 0.6%, and the Nasdaq jumped 1.5%. The Dow Jones Industrial Average slipped 0.4% over the week.
Trump cancels US envoy trip to Pakistan
Oil prices climbed in Asian trading on Monday after U.S.-Iran negotiations stalled over the weekend, with Brent crude rising to $108 per barrel.
The gains came as the Strait of Hormuz remained largely shut, disrupting global energy flows and raising concerns about prolonged supply constraints.
U.S. President Donald Trump on Saturday canceled a U.S. envoy's planned diplomatic trip to Pakistan aimed at advancing Iran war negotiations, saying Tehran could initiate talks if it wished.
The first round of peace talks, held two weeks ago in Islamabad and led on the U.S. side by Vice President JD Vance, had ended without a deal.
Investors await 'Mag 7' earnings this week
Investor focus now shifts to a crucial week for corporate earnings, with five of the so-called “Magnificent Seven” technology giants set to report results.
Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT), Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL), Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), and Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) are scheduled to release earnings on Wednesday, followed by Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) on Thursday.
Attention will also turn to the Federal Reserve’s policy decision on Wednesday, which could mark the final meeting led by Chair Jerome Powell before Kevin Warsh is expected to take over in May.

data.title: Iran’s Araghchi expected back in Pakistan after Oman trip as U.S.-Iran talks stall
data.description:
Investing.com - Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi is set to arrive back in Pakistan on Sunday night after holding diplomatic discussions in Oman, maintaining a delicate back-channel with Islamabad. This comes as hopes for progress with Washington remain low, following the breakdown of plans for a second round of negotiations.
Araghchi’s shuttle diplomacy
Araghchi left Pakistan on Saturday after presenting Pakistani mediators with what Iranian officials described as a workable framework for ending the two-month conflict. 
The Iranian delegation that accompanied him has returned to Tehran but is expected to fly back to Islamabad on Sunday night, before Araghchi travels on to Russia, Iran’s state-run IRNA news agency reported. 
In Oman, he held discussions on regional developments and spoke by phone with his Egyptian counterpart Badr Abdelatty and Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan, with both calls focusing on the ceasefire and the state of negotiations.
[[pro_promotion |
Get deeper insights on how the Iran war impacts global markets - InvestingPro
]]
Trump scraps Islamabad mission
The diplomatic activity follows President Donald Trump’s abrupt cancellation on Saturday of a planned trip to Islamabad by envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner. 
"We’re not going to spend 15 hours in airplanes all the time going back and forth to be giving a document that was not good enough, and so we’ll deal by telephone, and they can call us anytime they want," Trump told reporters near Air Force One in Florida.
Iran’s President Masoud Pezeshkian told Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif by phone that Tehran would not enter "imposed negotiations" under threats or blockade, and that Washington must first lift its port blockade before any groundwork for a deal could be laid. An Iranian diplomatic source told Reuters Tehran would not accept "maximalist demands."
Naval pressure
As of Saturday, 37 ships have been redirected by the US Navy, with three vessels seized for non-compliance since the blockade began in early April. A Navy helicopter from the USS Pinckney intercepted the M/V Sevan, one of 19 Treasury-sanctioned shadow fleet vessels, in the Arabian Sea, escorting it back toward Iran. 
The USS George H.W. Bush aircraft carrier has arrived in the Middle East, bringing the number of US carriers operating in the region to three. Fewer than five ships crossed the Strait of Hormuz in the past 24 hours, against a pre-war daily average of around 130.
Shooting shadows Trump’s evening
Trump also addressed a shooting at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner in Washington on Saturday night, where a suspect armed with a shotgun, handgun and multiple knives charged a Secret Service checkpoint at the Washington Hilton. One officer was struck but protected by a bulletproof vest. 
The suspect, has been taken into custody. Trump said the incident was unrelated to the Iran conflict. More than 5,000 people have been killed since the war began on February 28.
Simon Mugo and Rachael Rajan contributed to this report.

data.title: WHCD gunman likely targeted Trump administration, acting US attorney general says
data.description:
Investing.com - The gunman who opened fire at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner appeared to have deliberately targeted President Donald Trump and members of his administration, Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said on Sunday, transforming what initially appeared to be a chaotic security breach into what investigators are treating as a premeditated strike against the executive branch.
"It does appear that he did, in fact, set out to target folks that work in the administration, likely including the President," Blanche said on NBC News’ "Meet the Press."
The suspect - identified as Cole Thomas Allen, 31, a teacher from Torrance, California, is believed to have travelled by train from Los Angeles to Chicago to Washington before carrying out Saturday night’s attack at the Washington Hilton, Blanche said.
Investigators found writings linked to Allen, though Blanche declined to elaborate on their contents or confirm a motive.
Allen, a Caltech graduate holding a master’s degree in computer science and described by those who knew him as an award-winning tutor, opened fire with a shotgun and a handgun inside the hotel and was also found to be carrying knives. 
He was taken into custody at the scene and is scheduled to appear in federal court on Monday on charges of assault on a federal officer using a dangerous weapon and use of a firearm during a crime of violence.
Wounded in action
One Secret Service officer was struck by gunfire and remains in stable condition. Washington D.C. interim Police Chief Jeffery Carroll confirmed the agent was "in good spirits." Secret Service Director Sean Curran said Trump personally called the wounded officer to express his support.
U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia Jeanine Pirro said Allen appeared intent on inflicting maximum harm, but that a security checkpoint positioned just outside the ballroom, where thousands of guests had assembled to hear the president speak, had prevented further casualties. 
"Thankfully because of the checkpoint right outside the ballroom, there was no one who was injured," Pirro said.
Trump was evacuated swiftly by Secret Service agents after a loud concussive noise sent hundreds of journalists, politicians and celebrities diving for cover.
He later told reporters no federal officials had been seriously harmed and praised the rapid response of security personnel, adding that he had recommended the event carry on. "I have recommended that we ’LET THE SHOW GO ON,’" Trump posted on social media, adding that he would be guided by law enforcement.
Among those caught in the chaos was Harmeet Dhillon, head of the Justice Department’s civil-rights division, who sustained a head bruise and a cut to her nose after Secret Service agents surged across her table. She was subsequently evacuated by a U.S. Marshals team and thanked agents publicly on social media.
Public filings show Allen had no declared party affiliation, though records indicate a minor political donation in 2024. Investigators continue to probe his background and movements in the days leading up to the attack.
[[pro_promotion |
Track the market impact of domestic political volatility with InvestingPro
]]
Trump’s previous assassination attempts
Saturday’s incident is the third time Trump has faced what authorities have described as an assassination attempt since leaving office in 2021.
In July 2024, a gunman wounded him in the ear at a campaign rally in Butler, Pennsylvania. Months later, a man armed with a rifle was found hiding near Trump’s golf course in West Palm Beach, Florida; that suspect received a life sentence in February 2025.
Gun violence prevention organisation Everytown for Gun Safety condemned the attack. Its president John Feinblatt said the sight of the nation’s president and press corps taking cover was "absolutely unacceptable and we refuse to accept this as normal."
Simon Mugo contributed to this report.

data.title: Does the Fed care more about unemployment or inflation?
data.description:
Investing.com - The Federal Reserve, which operates under a dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment, appears to be tilting its focus back toward inflation after a period in which employment concerns had taken precedence, according to analysts at UBS Global Research. 
[[pro_promotion |
Access faster breaking news and analyst reactions with InvestingPro - up to 50% off
]]
UBS economist Arend Kapteyn found that the weight the Fed attributed to unemployment in setting policy had converged with, and marginally exceeded, its weight on inflation heading into early 2026, but that a stagflationary shock "emanating from the Middle East" is now shifting the balance back.
"Our impression is that the emphasis is shifting back a bit towards inflation, but confirmation will have to wait for the June Summary of Economic Projections," Kapteyn said.
The analysis examined how changes in the Fed’s dot plot, the Federal Open Market Committee’s projection for the appropriate policy rate, responded to revisions in its own inflation and unemployment forecasts through rolling 10-quarter regressions of Summary of Economic Projections data. 
The coefficients tracked the relative weight the Fed placed on each side of its mandate over time.
During the post-pandemic inflation surge, the Fed’s priority was unambiguously inflation control, a stance made easier by a labor market at or near full employment, which allowed tightening without breaching the employment side of the mandate. 
As that inflation episode faded, the Fed grew increasingly concerned about a weakening labor market, and the unemployment coefficient in the rolling regression rose while the inflation coefficient fell.
By early 2026, the two coefficients had converged, with the unemployment weighting sitting marginally above the inflation weighting, suggesting a broadly balanced Fed, neither decisively hawkish nor dovish in its mandate priorities.
The chart tracking the evolution of FOMC weighting from the third quarter of 2024 through the first quarter of 2026 shows the unemployment coefficient rising from near zero to above one, while the inflation coefficient fell from roughly two toward the same range. The lines crossed sometime in 2025 before settling into near-parity.
The methodology used is straightforward: if inflation projections rise while unemployment forecasts remain unchanged, yet the median funds rate projection does not move, that would indicate the Fed is placing less weight on inflation risks and leaning more dovish. The reverse would signal a hawkish tilt.
Kapteyn noted the stagflation scenario now facing the Fed is distinctly harder to navigate than the post-pandemic period, when strong growth gave policymakers room to tighten aggressively. 
A simultaneous rise in inflation and unemployment would force a direct tradeoff between the two sides of the mandate, a situation the Fed largely avoided during its 2022-2023 tightening cycle.
The June SEP will be the first formal test of how the FOMC resolves that tradeoff in its published projections.

data.title: Australia’s Wong to visit Japan, China, South Korea to discuss energy security
data.description: SYDNEY, April 26 (Reuters) - Australia’s Foreign Minister Penny Wong on Sunday said she would visit Japan, China, and South Korea this week for talks on energy security in the face of disruptions caused by the Middle East conflict. Wong said in a statement that meetings with her counterparts in the three countries would "help ensure we are coordinating effectively" amid upheaval on global energy markets. • Wong said in Tokyo she would meet Japan’s Foreign Minister Toshimitsu Motegito for talks on energy and fuel security as well as the Middle East conflict. • In Beijing, Wong said she would hold the eighth Australia-China Foreign and Strategic Dialogue with China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi. The talks would "progress the full range of our interests and manage our differences", she said. • Wong said in Seoul she would meet with South Korea’s Foreign ​Minister Cho Hyun, describing South Korea as "one of Australia’s most important sources of refined fuels". • Australia, which imports most of its fuel, has experienced localised shortages during the Middle East conflict, which began in February.

data.title: Supply Chain Pulse Check as Iran war enters 3rd month
data.description:
Investing.com - Global supply chains remain under strain more than 50 days into the Iran war, with air spot rates up roughly 30% and ocean freight benchmarks sharply higher versus pre-conflict levels, according to analysts at Bernstein.
[[pro_promotion |
Track breaking market moves with live headlines and analyst notes - up to 50% off
]]
The Shanghai Containerized Freight Index has risen 42% and the Drewry World Container Index 18% since mid-February, Bernstein said. 
Air cargo capacity has tightened after Qatar Airways and Emirates, together accounting for around 11% of global ton-kilometres in 2024, cut operations to 60% and 74% of normal levels respectively, with jet fuel costs nearly doubling. 
Some air lanes have seen rates nearly double, with the composite airfreight rate hitting roughly $3.30/kg in January 2026.
Ocean volumes fell 3% in January and February combined, Bernstein said, partly reflecting tough comparisons ahead of tariff announcements in April 2025. Only around 2% of global container traffic transits the Strait of Hormuz, with Dubai’s Jebel Ali the main port affected, limiting the war’s direct impact on container shipping versus tankers.
Container lines have continued routing via the Cape of Good Hope on Asia-Europe services. Bernstein said a large-scale near-term return to the Red Sea is unlikely given the Houthis’ alignment with Iran, delaying any capacity release. 
Industry supply growth is forecast at 2.4% in 2026, accelerating to 6.9% in 2027 and 9.6% in 2028, well ahead of structural demand.
In trucking, spot truckload rates excluding fuel rose 24% year-on-year in February, 19% in March, and 25% so far in April, driven by stricter government oversight of the trucking market and fuel-price pressures squeezing carriers. 
J.B. Hunt Transport Services said on its first-quarter earnings call: "As we moved through the first quarter, the freight environment felt meaningfully different than what we’ve operated in over the past several years," adding that "a predominantly supply driven freight recovery continued to gain steam."
In March and April, chargeable air cargo weight declined 5-10%, yet revenues grew in the low-to-mid teens year-on-year in April on higher yields reflecting fuel, insurance and staffing surcharges, Bernstein said.
March PMIs showed mixed signals: the U.S. at 52.7, China at 50.8, and Europe at 51.6, its highest in nearly three years, Bernstein said, citing ISM and Eurostat data.
Oil prices are up $25-$30 per barrel since the conflict began, feeding into higher transport costs across modes, Bernstein said.
On the equities side, Bernstein rates DSV “outperform” with a DKK 2,100 price target, citing unreflected DB Schenker synergies, and rates Maersk “underperform” with a DKK 10,700 target, citing a challenged container shipping outlook and a fleet orderbook that continues to expand. FedEx and UPS carry “outperform” ratings with targets of $470 and $130 respectively.

data.title: How much of a threat is inflation?
data.description:
Investing.com - The global economy is weathering yet another inflationary shock, this time from the blockage of the Strait of Hormuz, but structural labor market conditions suggest the damage will be limited, according to a BCA Research.
[[pro_promotion |
Access faster breaking news and analyst reactions with InvestingPro - up to 50% off
]]
CPI swaps are pricing in inflation of 3.2% in both the U.S. and the Euro Area over the next 12 months. If realized, consumer prices will have risen 3.9% annually in the U.S. and 3.5% in the Euro Area since 2020, overshooting central bank targets across all major developed economies except Japan.
U.S. tariffs are already pushing core goods PCE inflation roughly three percentage points above where it would otherwise be, BCA Research data shows. Short-dated market-based inflation expectations have risen, though longer-term expectations remain anchored.
The immediate threat of a wage-price spiral, the mechanism that entrenched inflation in the 1970s, appears low. 
Global wage growth has decelerated as labor markets returned toward balance, with the global jobs-workers gap narrowing sharply from its post-2019 highs. "The risk of a major overshoot in inflation over the next few years is quite low," the report said.
Without accelerating wage growth, higher inflation would simply erode real wages, forcing households to cut discretionary spending and dampening demand, a self-correcting dynamic that limits further price acceleration.
Looking further out, four structural forces shape the inflation outlook through the end of the decade.
Fiscal policy presents a two-sided risk. Gross government debt now exceeds GDP in the U.S., UK, France, Italy and Japan.
Rising debt can fuel inflation through larger primary deficits or eventual debt monetization, though public opposition to inflation, cited as the most important issue by 34% of U.S. respondents in YouGov polling, constrains central bank willingness to pursue that path. If that aversion holds, high debt levels could paradoxically become deflationary, forcing governments to raise taxes or cut spending.
Globalization’s disinflationary tailwind is fading. China’s integration into global trade drove U.S. consumer durable goods prices down nearly 40% between 1995 and 2020, per Bureau of Economic Analysis data. Trade frictions, pandemic dislocations and the current oil shock have put that trend in reverse.
Demographics present a U-shaped inflation path. Slower population growth initially suppresses investment demand, the so-called accelerator effect, but aging populations reduce the ratio of workers to consumers over time. Economic support ratios have already begun falling globally, most acutely in Japan, pointing to eventual upward price pressure.
AI investment is currently inflationary. U.S. spending on IT software and hardware as a share of GDP has surpassed its 2000 peak. 
Hyperscalers alone are projected to deploy $678 billion in capital expenditure in 2026, up from $412 billion in 2025, per Factset. Longer term, if AI productivity gains flow primarily to higher-income households, who save rather than spend, the disinflationary effect could dominate.
The brokerage adds that "the more dire inflationary outlooks" are unlikely to materialize given public aversion to inflation, which it characterizes as the binding constraint on central bank behavior.

data.title: Acting US attorney general says shooting likely targeted Trump administration officials
data.description: WASHINGTON, April 26 (Reuters) - U.S. Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said on Sunday that he believes that the alleged White House Correspondents’ Dinner shooter was targeting Trump administration officials. "It does appear that he did, in fact, have set out to target folks that work in the administration, likely including the President," Blanche told NBC News’ "Meet the Press," adding that the suspect likely traveled by train from Los Angeles to Chicago and then to Washington, D.C. The suspect will be formally charged in federal court on Monday with assault of a federal officer and discharging a firearm in an attempt to kill a federal officer, Blanche said. Blanche said the suspect was not cooperating with investigators as of Sunday morning. President Donald Trump and first lady Melania Trump were rushed out of the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner in Washington by Secret Service agents on Saturday night after a man opened fire on security personnel nearby. The man fired a shotgun at a Secret Service agent at a checkpoint in the Washington Hilton hotel before being tackled and arrested, officials have said.

data.title: Ericsson vs. Nokia: Which stock to own in 2026
data.description: The “market backdrop is unexciting,” says a new Morgan Stanley note, and firm analysts are expecting the entire mobile Radio Access Network (RAN) market to be “flattish.” The note discusses the 5% decline in the highly profitable North American market in Q1, and weighs in on which of Ericsson (ERIC) (ERIC-B) and Nokia Oyj (NOK) (NOKIA) stock is better to own in 2026.In the Telecom Equipment Space, Morgan Stanley prefers NOKIA as the stock to own in 2026.While ERIC has rallied to a five-year high after NOKIA’s rerating, Ericsson doesn’t have the same level of data center exposure and no AI/cloud revenues. Despite its best-in-class Networks business, the industry is facing headwinds from slow Telco spend and lack of pricing power, say the analysts.A number of factors drove ERIC’s decline in Q1. These include tougher comps as strong AT&T delivery benefits faded, a shift of priorities to fixed from mobile due to overall market headwinds, and Ericsson’s limited exposure to the modernisation of the US cellular network.“Gross margins in mobile networks have been extremely resilient at around 50% every quarter since Q1 2025,” the analysts said, despite the historically cyclical nature of the industry.Previously, margins were at a low of 41% in 2021, driven by significant capex cutback by North American Telcos. Ericsson has shown itself able to manage geographical revenue mix fluctuations well by making operational improvements.At this time too, despite increasing semiconductor input costs, management is confident in its guidance of 49-51% Q2 margins.The company is also more exposed to the Enterprise segment after the acquisition of Vonage (VG). The firm thinks that the synergy opportunity is uncertain, while the asset value has been written down by over 50%. This is a “heavily loss-making” enterprise.Due to unfavourable FX and weaker North America revenue momentum, Morgan Stanley has trimmed its revenues and EBITDA assumptions for ERIC by 3-5%. Higher than expected restructuring charges for the year have also caused the EPS estimate to be revised downwards.Morgan Stanley’s Equal-Weight rating and SEK 95 / $11 price target for ERIC remain unchanged as lower forecasts are offset by a roll-forward to 2027 forecasts and a stronger net cash position.That said, the firm has raised its bull case estimates to SEK 120/ $13, applying a higher EBIT multiple of 10.5x (vs 9.5x previously) to reflect the recent sector re-rating.The sector as a whole is seen as in line, and in the current environment, the firm prefers NOKIA within the space.

data.title: Bitcoin holds steady as institutional derivatives reach new milestone
data.description:
Investing.com - Bitcoin (BitfinexUSD) continues to show remarkable resilience, trading at $77,517.7 down 0.11% as of 22:45 ET (02:45 GMT) on Sunday morning despite a flurry of geopolitical and regulatory developments. 
President Trump’s sudden decision to cancel a diplomatic mission to Pakistan, originally intended for talks regarding the ongoing Iran conflict, triggered a brief bout of volatility, but the market’s reaction remained notably muted. 
[[pro_promotion |
Get premium crypto market insights with analyst comments on InvestingPro
]]
Traders appear to be treating the geopolitical risks as short-term noise, keeping their focus firmly on the underlying shifts in the digital asset’s institutional infrastructure.
A watershed moment for institutional derivatives
The most significant development this week lies in the rapid institutionalization of the Bitcoin market. 
Data from Volmex reveals that open interest for options linked to BlackRock’s iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT) has surpassed $27.6 billion, officially overtaking the volume of Bitcoin options on the long-standing offshore giant, Deribit. 
The above milestone underscores a fundamental transition: the U.S.-regulated, onshore market is no longer secondary to global offshore venues.
Institutional allocators noted that the depth and liquidity of the IBIT options market offer a critical tool for sophisticated hedging and income-generating strategies, such as covered calls. 
Positioning remains bullish, with call option activity indicating long-term targets equivalent to Bitcoin trading near $110,000, the rise of IBIT is effectively democratizing access to professional-grade leverage for regulated investors. 
Analysts view this maturation as a net positive for price discovery, providing a more stable foundation for the asset class.
Regulatory headwinds and the road ahead
As institutional adoption accelerates, the landscape for retail-facing infrastructure is tightening. 
Tennessee has become the second U.S. state, following Indiana, to enact a total ban on cryptocurrency ATMs, with Governor Bill Lee signing legislation that requires all kiosks to be removed or decommissioned by July 1, 2026. 
Legislators cited the FBI’s 2025 report of $142 million in scam-related losses in the state as the primary catalyst for the prohibition. 
As states increasingly clamp down on such high-risk access points, the broader trend remains clear: a push toward regulated, transparent, and institutional-grade participation, even as older, less secure gateways are phased out.
Crypto price today: altcoins edge higher
Most altcoins also traded higher on Sunday morning.
World no.2 crypto Ethereum was down 0.15% to $2,314.50.
World no. 3 crypto XRP fell 1.00% to $1.4214.
Cardano and Solana dropped 0.87% and 0.25%, respectively. 
Among meme tokens, Dogecoin also fell 0.89%.
